definition: The controller commits to actions planned from the current observation and executes them without using later feedback to correct the remaining actions.
The controller keeps observing during execution, compares what happened with what was expected, and updates future actions from that feedback.

OGG. During replanning, it turns the gap between expected and observed visual evolution into a corrective direction, nudging the generated action sequence back toward a recoverable trajectory without retraining the policy.

Online Gradient Guidance. A correction signal added during replanning: when execution has drifted, the model uses visual deviation to guide the next generated actions instead of blindly sampling a new chunk.

A generative modeling method that learns a continuous path from noise or an intermediate state to the target sample. Here the target sample is a future robot action sequence; OGG adds a corrective guide to that generation path.

The number of consecutive future actions planned and executed from one policy call. A larger h executes a longer chunk with fewer model calls, but stays open-loop longer and can accumulate errors; a smaller h replans more often, improving reactivity at higher inference cost.
